Performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance of the Fund may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Performance data current to the most recent month end may be obtained by visiting southeasternasset.com . The prospectus expense ratio before waivers is 1.29%. The Global Fund's expense ratio is subject to a contractual fee waiver to the extent the Fund's normal operating expenses (excluding interest, taxes, brokerage commissions and extraordinary expenses) exceed 0.95% of average net assets per year. This agreement is in effect through at least May 1, 2027, and may not be terminated before that date without Board approval.

A theme so far this year is that it has not been a good time to report a complicated quarter as a public company. Our investees that needed more explanation were overly punished by the market. To quantify this effect, stocks in the portfolio that each detracted more than 50 basis points individually combined for a negative impact of over 600 basis points. However, the value per share decline for these stocks was less than 150 basis points in aggregate. While we will always have winners and losers in any given quarter, this was an abnormally large mismatch between price and value change. Many of these companies had results that could be read as disappointing to short-term earnings per share, but there was little to no impact to the free cash flow (FCF) per share that we believe will materialize and/or is the consensus estimate for 2027+.

While this dynamic gives us comfort that we remain on the right track, we are not waiting for the market to be a weighing machine instead of a voting machine. We have stepped up our behind-the-scenes engagement with our investees and are optimistic some of this work (that could go public) will show results as the year goes on. We have already had one instance of that in the Longleaf Small-Cap Fund . At our investments at larger and/or controlled companies such as FedEx (FDX), IAC (IAC), Philips (PHG) and Exor (EXXRF) where minority shareholders have less say (in theory), we have solid relationships and constructive dialogue with management teams. We are encouraged that newer holdings like Regeneron (REGN), IDP Education (IDPUF) and Albertsons (ACI) are off to a good start on the People angle. On the other hand, where we have neither a company on offense nor strong partners, we have sold to concentrate in higher conviction holdings.

This was a relatively good quarter for the value index. It was especially good for the Big Oil part of the Big Banks, Oils and Pharmas that we have written about before , as large, unhedged oil majors were the biggest driver of the index. While the short-term earnings power of Exxon (XOM) and others went up materially in the quarter, we do not think the long-term value per share of these companies – as defined by the futures curve and common sense – went up 40%. Another thing to consider is that those value investors who made the decision to pay higher multiples for growth over the last 10+ years generally had rougher first quarters, whereas those who have been more broadly diversified and focused on paying low multiples of current earnings did better in the quarter. We have a more eclectic approach than either extreme and delivered returns in between over this limited period. The average, non-energy “value stock” that trades at a low multiple of current earnings and did not do anything notable this quarter remained flattish, which beat the MSCI World index. As we have said before, just because something trades at a low multiple on readily determined earnings, that does not mean it is undervalued, nor does it qualify on Business and People, which come before Price for us.

Our better relative performance in the later, more intense selloff part of the quarter rhymed a lot with what happened almost exactly one year ago around “Liberation Day” when we outperformed. Much like how the tariff issue went down the list of investor concerns as '25 went on, it is possible that we have peace in the Middle East and everything works out well with private credit over the next year or two. It is also possible (or likely) that these two issues (and potentially others) are not as “correctable” as tariffs were. We are not positioned overly defensively via a top-down call, but rather because the market is paying us to own under-recognized quality companies on offense. Along those lines, it is worth calling out some companies that did not rise to contributor status as we define it below but did have solid quarters. For example, the related group of IAC (IAC) and MGM Resorts (MGM) both continued to generate FCF and sell non-core assets, while rumored bids for what we believe to be inferior Caesars Entertainment (CZR) suggest a much higher price for MGM Resorts and by extension IAC. CNX (CNX), with its more hedged earnings stream vs. other, lower-quality energy companies, only saw its stock price go up 5% in the quarter, but we think the long-term value of safe, USA natural gas went up more than that. Jollibee (JBFCF) announced a split of its assets that we believe will unlock value. Regeneron (REGN) and Philips (PHG) reported solid quarters while other healthcare companies likely overpaid for assets. We also think it unlikely that valuation gaps such as the public market multiple disparity between Albertsons (ACI) and Kroger (KR) and/or the public/private market difference between Magnum Ice Cream (MICC) and Froneri will persist long-term.

Notable Contributors & Detractors

FedEx (FDX) – Global logistics company FedEx was a contributor for the quarter. The stock responded to another quarter of strong execution, led by continued momentum at the Federal Express (FEC) segment, where volume growth, yield growth, and cost control again translated into meaningful profit growth. FEC delivered its most profitable peak holiday season ever, with margins expanding for a sixth consecutive quarter as more volume flowed through Network 2.0-optimized facilities and management continued to move the business toward higher-value B2B verticals. FedEx Freight remains on track to be spun off on June 1st and will be well positioned to benefit from a recovery in industrial demand as an independent company. Even after the stock price move, we continue to believe the market underappreciates the earnings power of the core parcels business and the value that can be unlocked through the Freight separation.

Glanbia (GLAPY) – Irish nutrition, ingredients and dairy company Glanbia contributed for the quarter. Shares held up well in a difficult market environment, though the stock continues to trade at undemanding multiples relative to global food and beverage staples peers. Full year 2025 was the last set of results burdened by the dilutive drag from the SlimFast and Body & Fit businesses before they were divested, and the business now enters 2026 with a better growth rate and more focus. Strategic optionality remains with the potential for the company to monetize its non-core dairy business.

Rayonier (RYC)/PotlatchDeltic (PCH) – Timberland companies Rayonier and PotlatchDeltic completed their merger in the quarter to become our largest single position in the Fund. While the combination of the pre-merger-close price move up in PotlatchDeltic and the post-merger-close price move down in Rayonier did not combine to make this a large detractor, it is worth noting the post-merger weakness we believe did not line up with the solid value creation opportunity at a company like this. While some of the sell-off was likely short-term/technical (index moves), timberland remains a great and long-term store of value. Interest rate moves and housing market sentiment can impact perception in the short term, but this asset class has historically been an inflation hedge that has performed well in a variety of environments. Post-merger, new Rayonier has a strong balance sheet and the ability to go on offense. We believe that targeted asset sales and share repurchase can drive value per share growth from here. We also wrote about the company in our most recent Research Perspectives note .

Canal+ (CNALF) – French pay-TV operator Canal+ was a detractor for the quarter. The stock's performance was a quarter of two halves. The shares rallied meaningfully in January following a substantive synergy update in which management put hard numbers behind the value creation opportunity from integrating MultiChoice. We trimmed our holdings into this strength. Those gains were fully retraced and then some following the FY25 results announcement in March, which saw solid 2025 results but 2026 guidance signalling a year of deliberate investment ahead of more substantial earnings growth over the following three years. Mr. Market reacted poorly to the short-term earnings disappointment, pushing the stock to levels below the price in June 2025. This was a time when, by any fundamental measure, the business was considerably less attractive with a much wider range of outcomes – e.g. the VAT settlement with French authorities then remained unresolved (since clarified and better than expected), cost inflation from UEFA exclusivity rights renewal was an open question (renewed in November 2025, at a lower-than-expected renewal cost), and the MultiChoice deal was not yet closed. The conclusion of the bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery this year also implies a healthy value for Canal+ well above where it trades currently. We have been adding again (alongside insider buying) at what we consider an attractive P/V.

Delivery Hero (DHER)(DHERO) – A global food delivery platform, was a detractor for the quarter. The company reported largely inline FY25 results with FCF delivery stronger than our and street expectations. Beyond the Middle East conflict (the company operates in certain countries in the region) and short-term competitive pressure in a few regions, the stock has been weak due to uncertainty around its ongoing strategic review and the fate of Prosus's (PROSY) 27% ownership of the company. Deliver Hero announced the sale of its Taiwan operations to Grab Holdings for $600 million (at 0.35X Gross Merchandise Value (GMV), much higher than our value and 0.13X GMV where DHER trades) and while this was billed as a “decisive first step” in the ongoing strategic review, we and others want to see more action on additional asset sales. We added to our position as the valuation became even more attractive. There are many like-minded shareholders on the case, and we expect some of our key concerns to be addressed in coming months.

IDP Education (IDPUF)– The leading international student placement agency and English testing service provider, was a detractor for the quarter. IDP reported solid results in a tough environment. Student placement volumes were down 25% year-over-year (YoY) in 1H driven by declines in markets like Canada and the UK. Despite this volume decline, student placement yield (revenue per student placed) was up 15%, demonstrating IDP's value proposition of bringing high quality international students to universities. English language testing volume was down 7% YoY driven by weak student flows from India, but revenue per test was up 8%. The company is on track to deliver 25 million AUD in net cost reduction in FY26 (quite meaningful for a business expected to deliver 125 million AUD in operating income this year). Despite this strong execution, the stock has been weak as the market is concerned about the growth outlook in FY27 given adverse immigration policy changes in key destination markets (UK, Canada and Australia) in recent years. After an over 50% decline in student volumes from post Covid highs, we believe the pendulum has swung too far to the negative and expect placement volume declines to slow down to mid-single-digit percentages in FY27, such that we expect IDP to deliver on its commitment of profitable and cash generative growth in FY27 and beyond. We added to our investment as the stock price pulled back to highly attractive levels.

Mattel (MAT) – Children's toy, media, and consumer products creator Mattel was a detractor in the quarter. The stock fell due to an unexpected $150 million (15% of EBITDA) in incremental spending on initiatives including mobile gaming, Brick Shop (Mattel's competitor to LEGO), and direct to consumer marketing. CEO Ynon Kreiz cited a one-year payback on this spend, but the market remains in show-me mode and the price declined proportionate to the 2026 earnings per share guidance reduction. This was compounded by 4Q results that missed expectations, especially in the US. To the positive, the company committed to $1.5 billion in share repurchase over the next 3 years, which equates to 33% of shares outstanding at today's price. 2026 should have demonstrated the true FCF power of Mattel given traction on IP initiatives including two new movies (Masters of the Universe and Matchbox), two new mobile game launches, and licensing momentum with Toy Story 5 and KPop Demon Hunters. Unfortunately, this was delayed, and we are focused on what we can do to improve this situation.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter we had no new purchases and exited four holdings: Bio-Rad (BIO), Louis Hachette (LSHGF), Louisiana-Pacific (LPX) and Walt Disney (DIS). We exited underweight positions in both Bio-Rad and Louis Hachette in order to focus on holdings that are better positioned qualitatively and quantitatively, as we suggested we would in our fourth quarter letter. We exited a small, brief and successful investment in Louisiana-Pacific after housing market optimism early in the year led the share price to approach our appraisal value. We also sold Walt Disney (DIS) earlier in the quarter as it was one of our higher P/V and more economically sensitive investments, but we hope to get a chance to invest in the company again.

Outlook

Looking ahead, the war in Iran remains a source of uncertainty for a few holdings, but we do not own businesses that depend on a single outcome for success. More broadly, we believe the portfolio is positioned to benefit as potential private credit stress exposes weaker balance sheets and excess leverage, which our portfolio has little direct exposure to either. We continue to see unique opportunities in the portfolio driven by companies on offense with the help of Southeastern, and the portfolio remains attractively priced with the price-to-value ratio in the mid-50s%.

Thank you for your partnership.

See following page for important disclosures.

Before investing in any Longleaf Partners Fund, you should carefully consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For a current Prospectus and Summary Prospectus, which contain this and other important information, visit https://regdocs.blugiant.com/longleaf/# . Please read the Prospectus and Summary Prospectus carefully before investing.

Risks

The Longleaf Partners Global Fund is subject to stock market risk, meaning stocks in the Fund may fluctuate in response to developments at individual companies or due to general market and economic conditions. Also, because the Fund generally invests in 15 to 25 companies, share value could fluctuate more than if a greater number of securities were held. Investing in non-US securities may entail risk due to non-US economic and political developments, exposure to non-US currencies, and different accounting and financial standards. These risks may be higher when investing in emerging markets.

The MSCI World Index captures large and mid-cap representation across 23 Developed Markets (DM) countries. With 1,320 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each country. The MSCI World Value Index captures large and mid cap securities exhibiting overall value style characteristics across 23 DM countries. The value investment style characteristics for index construction are defined using three variables: book value to price, 12-month forward earnings to price and dividend yield. DM countries include: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hong Kong, Ireland, Israel, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the UK and the US. The S&P 500 Index is an index of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity and industry grouping, among other factors. The S&P is designed to be a leading indicator of US equities and is meant to reflect the risk/return characteristics of the large cap universe. Indexes are unmanaged, do not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses and cannot be invested in directly.

P/V ("price to value") is a calculation that compares the prices of the stocks in a portfolio to Southeastern's appraisal of their intrinsic values. The ratio represents a single data point about a holding and should not be construed as something more. P/V does not guarantee future results, and we caution investors not to give this calculation undue weight.

"Margin of Safety" is a reference to the difference between a stock's market price and Southeastern's calculated appraisal value. It is not a guarantee of investment performance or returns.

Free Cash Flow (FCF) is a measure of a company's ability to generate the cash flow necessary to maintain operations. Generally, it is calculated as operating cash flow minus capital expenditures.

Price / Earnings (P/E) is the ratio of a company's share price compared to its earnings per share.

PE multiple is a financial metric that frames a company's current stock price in terms of the company's earnings per share.

A Basis Point is one hundredth of one percent.

EBITDA is a company's earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization.

Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A) are business transactions in which the ownership of a company, business organization, or one of their operating units is transferred to or consolidated with another entity.

As of March 31, 2026, the top ten holdings for the Longleaf Partners Global Fund: Rayonier, Inc. 8.1%; IAC, Inc. 7.9%; EXOR N.V. 7.0%; Canal+ S.A. 6.5%; Mattel, Inc. 6.3%; CNX Resources Corporation 5.8%; Albertsons Companies, Inc. 5.0%; Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, Inc. 4.8%; Magnum Ice Cream Company N.V. 4.6% and Delivery Hero S.E. 4.5%. data.title: Private Credit Crisis Is Not The 2008 Subprime Crisis
data.description:
Earlier this week, the Federal Reserve reportedly asked US banks' about their exposure to private credit. Recent developments in the private credit market are compared by some analysts to the subprime market while other analysts downplay any impact. Which one makes more sense? In this article we will delve into whether the private credit market could turn out into another 2008 subprime crisis.

Hyman Minsky: The Theory Behind Financial Crises

For those of us that have been through the so-called Great Financial Crisis of 2008 (a period I remember as if it was still yesterday), Hyman Minsky was an economist that was widely quoted during that difficult period in time.

Without going into too many esoteric details, Hyman Minsky had many timeless observations about why financial cries were recurring.

Basically, he divided finance into three types:

  • 1.Interest and principal on debt can be fully repaid from cashflows.
  • 2. only the interest on debt can be fully repaid and the principal needs to be rolled over.
  • 3. neither the interest nor principal on debt can be repaid and relies on continuous borrowing or asset sales just to pay interest.

His critical insight, in my view, is a paradox: a stable economy creates the conditions for financial instability. In a stable economy, optimism grows and lenders and borrowers become complacent. Finance shifts from the first type to the second type and increasingly the third type. Success breeds more risk taking until you have large swaths of money committed to increasingly risky investments/loans that rely on asset appreciation just to be economically viable. A “unexpected” black swan that leads to default on interest payments snowballs into a rush for the exits as investors that were hungry for yield suddenly get cold feet and want their principal back, precipitating a financial crisis.

Put it another way, good times lead to investors overreaching for yield. Since no one can tell when the good times will end, this overreaching for yield accumulates risks that will go bad in a market downturn. This issue is multiplied by institutions that get some sort of fee based off volume (e.g. a percentage of assets under management or new originations) and are more concerned with meeting quarterly growth targets met by increasing bets in the risky sector until the last investment dollar is squeezed dry. These institutions and the people that run them are less worried about losses accumulating as they will have made large fees and profits during the good times. This is encapsulated in the famous quote from Citibank then-CEO Chuck Prince in July 2007 “as long as the music is on, we’re still dancing”. When the bad times inevitably hit, investors and eventually the public are left holding the bag.

Subprime crisis of 2007-2008, then

Before we get into the current day private credit market, let’s flashback to the 2007-2008 subprime crisis (which then morphed into the Great Financial Crisis of 2008) and see Minsky’s theory in action.

Let’s start with two charts below. The first chart shows how interest rates were slashed from 6.5% to 1% after the dotcom bubble burst. The second chart shows how housing prices had been steadily appreciated for several decades. An often heard quote back in 2006 was that there has never been a year where housing prices declined nationally in the US (I’m not sure how far back people were looking at that time as housing prices probably did decline during the Great Depression, some studies suggest this was a valid belief during 1975-2006).

Low interest rates and an appreciating mainstream asset coupled together to create an explosion in mortgage origination activity and money flooded into the subprime sector (i.e. loans to borrowers with low credit scores). While this may have worked on a small scale as in 1994, only $35 billion in subprime mortgages were originated (about 104,000 loans), by 2007 there were 7.5 million subprime loans outstanding (accounting for about 14% of mortgages at that time), with another 8-10% of “near-prime” loans. Buffett calls it the three Is, the innovator, the imitator and the idiot. The innovator may have found an opportunity in mispriced subprime loans in the 1990s, which was massively replicated by the imitators in the 2000s when interest rates were near zero and housing was appreciated.

Long story short, companies deep in the subprime trough went bust left and right. If we look back, 2008 seems like just a blip in the stock market’s upward trajectory. Ironically, even some of the subprime assets might not have been that bad. I remember when the Federal Reserve purchased $30 billion in mortgage assets from Bear Stearns in 2008 to provide liquidity. By the 2010s, the Federal Reserve actually managed to exit these assets at a profit (though probably in part due to the monetary easing creating a conducive environment in those years).

Alas but this was too late for many of the big names in subprime. Bear Stearns’ stock price fell to $2/share, while AIG is still trading at a fraction of where it was 2008. Value lost by shareholders/investors in the companies most exposed to subprime was real.

A major reason in my opinion is leverage. Subprime products were often leveraged in several ways: they were divided into tranches by risk (senior AAA tranches) and mezzanine and equity tranches which absorbed losses first. Then CDOs would repackage mezzanine tranches into senior AAA tranches and riskier mezzanine tranches. And given the underlying assets (subprime mortgages) typically had very high loan-to-values (LTV), you can see how these products were inherently fragile. In fact, had there been no leverage and banks just held an unleveraged subprime mortgage to maturity, there might not be much losses in the long run – using Shiller US national home price index as a proxy, 2008 was 180, now it’s 330, so home prices increased by 83%. Even if all the loans defaulted and the banks repossessed all the property, it’d be up 83% by now and probably could cover the loans. As Warren Buffett said, there are three Ls in losing money, leverage, liquor and I’ll omit the last L so it doesn’t sound misogynist, but he emphasized leverage.

Private Credit, Now

Back from our detour down the memory lane to the present day. Private credit has expanded rapidly since the Global Financial Crisis, growing from around $300 billion AUM in 2007 to nearly $2.5 trillion by 2023 (as of now, this figure is estimated by many sources to be near $3.5 trillion).

Let’s take a deep dive into one of the biggest names in the private credit space. Note that by analyzing this asset management company and its listed BDC, I am not necessarily thinking they are doing better or worse than any other companies in the field, but they are prominent and focused in this sector, so any analysis will be more relevant to private credit than some big asset manager that only has dabbled few percentage of its AUM in private credit.

Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL)

OWL is one of the biggest names in private credit with $300 billion AUM (of which about $158 billion AUM is in credit platforms, e.g. direct lending). Its stock price has fallen a lot throughout 2025 as investors' worries over OWL grew.

It mainly earns investment management fees from its funds. It has managed to grow its revenues from $200 million in 2019-2020 to $2.9 billion in 2025.

Let’s take a look at its biggest listed platform Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) to see what’s under the hood (below is from 10-k)

As at December 2025

Balance

Corresponding Income statement (FY2025)

Assets

$16.4 billion

Interest & investment income: $1.8 billion

Debt

$9.3 billion

Interest expense: -$0.6 billion

Net assets

$7.4 billion

FY2025

Income statement

Interest & investment income

$1.8 billion

Interest expense

-$0.6 billion

Management & incentive fees

-$0.4 billion

Net investment income (before fair value changes)

$0.8 billion

Source:10-K

So what we see is that OBDC earns $1.8 billion interest and investment income off $16.4 billion in assets, about 11%. Return on equity (using net investment income divided by net assets) is about 11% as well. Interest expense is roughly at 6.5% of debt.

But what if OBDC were entirely unlevered, assuming management and incentive fees changed proportionately with interest and investment income?

FY25

Interest & investment income

$0.8 billion ($7.4 billion net assets *11%)

Management & incentive fees

-$0.2 billion (FY25 0.4 billion fees/1.8 revenues*0.8 billion revenues))

Net investment income

$0.6 billion

Source: 10-K

The ROE would declined to about 8%, which would be at comparable levels with high yield bonds (one reference index puts the effective yield of high yield bonds at 7.14%). So effectively OBDC is juicing up returns with leverage so it generates 11% ROE on paper (and a higher management fee for OWL as compared to if unleveraged), otherwise it would look very similar to a high-yield bond ETF. This business model has worked very well for OWL. OBDC assets grew from $1 billion in Dec-2016 to $17 billion by Dec-25 and OWL’s assets under management and fees have grown as well.

On the other hand, OBDC’s underlying assets don’t seem to me to be too bad:

  • it basically makes loans to middle market companies (e.g. associated with during takeovers by private equity).
  • As of December 31, 2025, the portfolio companies “had weighted average annual revenue of $1.01 billion, weighted average annual EBITDA of $237 million, an average interest coverage of 1.9x and an average net loan-to value of 42%”. So there is some margin of safety embedded in these deals, though OBDC caveats that “The debt in which we invest typically is not rated by any rating agency, but if these instruments were rated, they would likely receive a rating of below investment grade (that is, below BBB- or Baa3), which is often referred to as “high yield” or “junk.””
  • The investments are also spread across a wide range of industries, even though software has the highest concentration, it is only 11.1% of total.

There’s always going to be a bit of irrational exuberance during upcycles, but OBDC’s underlying assets appear to me to be better than the subprime loans being made during the 2000s (such as NINJA (no income, no job or assets) loans). Even though the private credit segment has been facing redemptions and forced to put a cap on these redemptions, thereby fueling more investor angst, this is still somewhat different from the subprime crisis where banks owned oodles of subprime mortgage securities, were taking write-offs and people were worried about which bank would go under next. In the private credit universe, some companies may do poorly and even go under, but it’s all ringfenced amongst the investors in those securities and while it may dampen investor enthusiasm for a few quarters or even years, the impact is much more concentrated and narrow than the subprime crisis and the next time the Fed embarks on an easing cycle, investors will gradually forget about it and move on to the next big thing.

Nonetheless, I would not suggest new investment into OWL or OBDC or other private credit instruments and related companies. It is very difficult to know when these cycles bottom, while many saw there was something going on the subprime mortgages, few could predict it would end with Lehman’s bankruptcy and a whole string of other companies — Federal National Mortgage Association (FNMA) aka Fannie Mae, American International Group, Inc. (AIG), etc — losing tremendous amounts of value for shareholders.

When Retail Goes In…

What might have been a good idea on a small scale was widely replicated via Buffett’s 3Is in the past decade, with even retail participation.

I’m a bit skeptical of “private equity” opportunities offered to retail investors:

  • retail investors are not offered the chance to invest in some of the biggest tech names during their growth phase before they go public, e.g. retail investors will only be able to buy into SpaceX at a reported IPO valuation of $1.75 trillion, after private investors have already benefited from most of the low-hanging fruit
  • private equity management companies going public have actually historically been signs of market peaks as excellently captured below. While this does not have anything to do with private credit directly, it’s similar in essence. Private equity makes money off deals and I’d be wary if they are selling you their a piece of their deal-making prowess.

  • and yet, in recent years we’ve heard about “private” opportunities becoming more open to retail investors which piques my wariness rather than enthusiasm as retail investors are not getting a piece of the action for SpaceX but somehow are offered PE fund buy-out loans in a leveraged structure such as OBDC.

Conclusion

If we look at OWL and OBDC as representative of the private credit sector, it does not appear to as systemic and pervasive a risk to the broader market as subprime mortgages were. The headlines will look scary, a few leveraged investments may go bad, but it’ll largely be contained within the investors of those instruments (which will have to sit through a few agonizing years hoping the money they invested eventually comes back and some may suffer losses).

How bad could it be?

  • Let's use the historical annual average default rate of 4.1% for speculative grade fixed income investments from S&P, and let's assume a 50% loss recovery.
  • Let's assume 5 years' of losses (as private credit grew a lot in the past 5 years) are concentrated in 1 or 2 years as people clamor for redemptions and credit tightens.
  • Back of the envelope math suggests 4%*50%*5= about 10% losses, multiplied by $3.5 trillion private credit AUM, would be about $350 billion in losses (c.1% of US nominal GDP of $30 trillion) By comparison, subprime loan losses were $1 trillion during 2007-2009 (7% of US nominal GDP of $14 trillion in 2007). Private credit would have lose $2 trillion (or about 57% of total AUM) in order to have the same magnitude impact as subprime and even if I don't find products like OBDC attractive, I don't think they are going to suffer anywhere near an average 57% loss on assets across the industry in every product.
  • To sum up, it'll probably be another blip in the credit cycle where inflows slow down for a few quarters as investors nurse their losses, but even if a few hundred billion dollars are lost, it's not going to be the end of the world.

However, I think retail investors should be very cautious in investing in these instruments as OBDC appears to be using leverage to increase returns which would otherwise be only somewhat better than a high yield bond. Even if say 10% of the loan value eventually are not recovered, with 2X leverage that's a 20% hit to capital. If investor appetite dries up, OWL's revenues could fall significantly. As the subprime crisis illustrated, permanent losses for investors can happen to specific companies in the impacted sector (especially with high leverage), so individual investors might want to think twice before deploying capital to this sector.

In my opinion, the best way for retail investors to play this is not to do anything in this sector and buy broader market equities, such as (SPY) or (QQQ) or financial sector ETF (XLF) if there is actually a "private credit crisis" blared all over the news as markets seize up on short term liquidity shortage, while in the long run (as in the subprime crisis) the Fed will print to ease any liquidity shortage.

data.title: Climb Bio: 'Buy' On Budoprutug Phase 2 Advancement pMN And Expansions Underway
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Climb Bio, Inc. (CLYM), it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Eliem Therapeutics: Year End 2024 MN Data With Further Potential To Unlock Value." Before going further on this biotech, it is important to highlight that in that article I mentioned that the company had acquired Tenet Medicines and also had obtained a $120 million private placement agreement to carry it forward. The reason why I bring this up is because it decided to switch over its pipeline afterwards only to advance budoprutug [TNT119] and other drugs for the treatment of patients with autoimmune disorders. The end result is that it changed its name from Eliem Therapeutics to Climb Bio because of this strategic move on October 3, 2024.

It has been a while since I updated this biotech, but at that time I had given it a Buy rating based on its potential to develop the use of its anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody [mAb] budoprutug for the treatment of patients with primary membranous nephropathy [pMN] in an ongoing phase 1b study. More about the clinical data below, but the company did end up actually releasing positive results from its phase 1b study using this drug to treat these autoimmune disorder patients. Even better, it is currently in the process of evaluating the use of budoprutug in the ongoing phase 2 PrisMN trial treating these patients with pMN. Why do I believe it is important to go over this biotech now? That's because there are actually several catalysts or inflection points that are rapidly approaching. Initial data from the PrisMN trial is expected in the 2nd half of 2026.

That's not all, either; this drug is also being explored in other studies targeting patients with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus [SLE] and immune thrombocytopenia [ITP] as well, with clinical readouts from both of these programs anticipated in the 2nd half of 2026 as well. Another item to mention is that budoprutug is versatile in the sense that the company wants to shift it away eventually from intravenous [IV] dosing over to a subcutaneous [SC] formulation. The purpose is to provide patients with a self-administered at-home treatment option eventually. Data from a phase 1 healthy volunteer study evaluating the SC formulation of budoprutug are expected in the 1st half of 2026. With budoprutug still pressing forward in clinical testing targeting patients with pMN, plus several data readouts expected ahead, I believe it is important to maintain a Buy rating on this stock at this time.

Primary Membranous Nephropathy Indication Program Continues To Deliver Necessary Data

As I mentioned above, the company changed its name to Climb Bio, and the reason for doing so was to shift its focus for its pipeline to the targeting of patients with autoimmune disorders. With that being said, the company was indeed able to report positive results from its phase 1b study using budoprutug for the treatment of patients with primary membranous nephropathy [pMN]. It was even able to exhibit long-term data showing control of proteinuria in 4 patients three years after initial dosing [with a total of 4 doses]. It is even in the process of running the phase 2 open-label, dose-range-finding PrisMN study using this anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody to treat pMN patients who have persistent proteinuria despite optimized renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system [RAAS] inhibition. Before going over this entire pMN treatment program in detail, plus any catalysts to come about from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Primary Membranous Nephropathy [pMN] is characterized as an autoimmune disorder of the kidney. Specifically, the immune system [primary anti-PLA2R antibodies] attacks the glomeruli [tiny filters] of the kidney. Why is that bad? That's because these filters are responsible for filtering out waste from the blood of a patient. Without these being effective, there are two main issues that these patients experience:

  • Reduced kidney function
  • Protein in the urine.

With respect to the second one, protein in the urine, a little bit is okay. However, these patients can experience something known as Nephrotic Syndrome, which is protein in the urine above 3.5 g/day. This is a cascading effect because then this syndrome can lead to swelling in multiple parts of a person's body. The majority, 3 out of 4 people with MN, have pMN, which is what this company is going after. The other type, Secondary Membranous Nephropathy is a result of an infection and/or other autoimmune disorder like lupus, possibly. The global membranous nephropathy market is expected to reach $590 million by 2035. This initial market is not as big as other multibillion-dollar markets, but there is something of importance to mention here. Should the company be successful in developing budoprutug for pMN, it could potentially have the whole market to itself. The reason why is because there are no FDA-approved therapies for this rare kidney disease.

These patients desperately need a new treatment option to control proteinuria and the many other problems. However, there is something more important to note, which is that uncontrolled pMN disease means the patient is moving on to developing chronic kidney disease or end-stage kidney disease. The best that patients could hope for would be the current 1st-line treatment option for them, which is rituximab. There are two problems with this treatment option, though. The first of which is that about between 20% and 40% of patients who receive it are refractory [Climb Bio Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #14].

Secondly, it is only given as an off-label option. As I mentioned above, the beauty of budoprutug is that it can be applied to many disorders. The other two disorders that the company is looking at treating with this drug are SLE and ITP, both of which would offer expansion potential. This is only made possible, though, because of the fact that this anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody has broad coverage. That is, it is able to deplete pathogenic B-cells across the entire B-cell lineage [Climb Bio Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #11]. Including that of Ab-secreting B cells, which are plasmablasts and plasma cells. With its 100X enhanced antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity [ADCC] and its strong binding affinity to CD19, this drug is well equipped to help treat pMN patients.

The thing is that this company has been able to show this drug to actually work well in treating these patients. This would be with both phase 1b data and long-term data. The phase 1b data was ideal because the final outcome was that 100% [5 out of 5] ended up achieving rapid and durable B-cell depletion with a dose of budoprutug ranging from 100 mg to 200 mg. This is crucial because it goes back to what I stated above, which is that B-cell depletion is necessary to achieve desired data. This is exactly what transpired in that the final outcome was that all 5 patients had also achieved either a complete remission or partial remission in the same study at Week 48. This was measured to look at proteinuria, known as Urine Protein Creatine Ratio [UPCR]. The second aspect to consider here would be long-term data. Climb Bio also delivered on this as well because it was noted that pMN patients were able to achieve long-term control of proteinuria for 3 years after initial dosing [up to 4 doses given for them].

Furthermore, the notion is that 3 of these 4 patients no longer required immunosuppressive therapy either. Why is this finding important? That's because such therapy is given to these patients to prevent attacks from anti-PLA2R that damage the kidney's filtration barrier.

It remains to be seen what happens with this program, but it definitely is an unmet medical need. That is why the company also achieved another piece of good news lately, which is that the FDA granted budoprutug for the treatment of patients with pMN with Fast Track Designation. This is because of the fact that there are no therapies approved for these patients, plus the early data observed to date from it showing the potential to be a disease-modifying therapy for them.

In order to see whether or not Climb Bio is going to be able to effectively use this drug to treat these patients, it is evaluating its use in the ongoing phase 2 open-label, dose-range-finding PrisMN study. The thing is that there are two criteria for the patients that are being recruited into this study. The first of which is that they have to be anti-PLA2R antibody-positive. Secondly, they must have persistent proteinuria despite optimized RAAS inhibition. Such pMN patients who still have ≥1 g/day despite RASS inhibition are at greater risk for kidney failure. What is this inhibition? Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system [RAAS] inhibitors are important for reducing intracapillary glomerular pressure and offering size selectivity as well. Both of these events are supposed to help reduce proteinuria. The company is going to treat these patients in a dose-escalation design as follows:

  • 15 patients on low-dose
  • 15 patients mid-dose
  • 15 patients on high dose.

Each of these cohorts is to be given IV budoprutug as two doses 14 days apart. From there, they are to receive a maintenance dose at 24 weeks [two doses again]. From there, they will be evaluated for the main efficacy endpoint of complete and partial remission at Week 48 [Both UPCR and eGFR are being deployed for this endpoint]. As I mentioned above, what's also important to track would be biomarker data. This deals with depleted B-cells, anti-PLA2R negativity, and immunoglobulin. With that being said, Climb Bio expects to release data from the phase 2 PrisMN trial of budoprutug treating patients with pMN in the 2nd half of 2026.

The main thing to note with the data is that it is only going to be initial data. This means the intention is to release B-cell and anti-PLA2R data only. Again, the drug can be used for other autoimmune disorders. With that being said, data from phase 1b studies using this anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody to treat patients with SLE and ITP are expected in the 2nd half of 2026 as well. Plus, the company is already thinking about how it could provide a competitive advantage with respect to dosing. It is evaluating this drug in a phase 1 human volunteer study to test an SC formulation of budoprutug in terms of pharmacokinetic [PK], pharmacodynamic [PD], and bioavailability profiles. Although, the truth is that this company already has a highly competitive profile to begin with [Climb Bio Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #20].

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC filing, Climb Bio had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $160.7 million as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that this biotech is in great shape in terms of its cash on hand. The reason why I state this is because in its Q4 and full-year 2025 financial results, it mentions that it believes its cash on hand is enough to fund its operations into 2028. Thus, I don't believe that there will be the need to raise cash in the short term.

With that said, it is also important to note that there are several catalysts expected to happen during this year. This means that if positive results are released, causing the stock price to trade considerably higher, then it is my opinion that management might take advantage of this and raise cash anyway. There even is a financial tool already in place for this company at its disposal, should it choose to utilize it. This would be an Equity Distribution Agreement with Oppenheimer, whereby the company could sell from time to time up to an aggregate of $22.4 million.

The company burns about $19.3 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $13.7 million in R&D expenses and then $5.6 million in G&A expenses.

Risks to Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Climb Bio. The first risk to consider would be in terms of its lead program, which is using budoprutug for the treatment of patients with pMN in the ongoing phase 2 PrisMN study. As I have gone over above, partial or complete remission was observed in 100% of patients [5 out of 5] at Week 48. The same primary endpoint of remission will also be evaluated in the ongoing PrisMN trial.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that the remissions observed in the phase 1b study will be again observed in the next phase 2 study. Especially since there was a small sample size released from the phase 1b study.

The second risk to consider would be the expansion opportunities that the company is going after with its development of anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody budoprutug. It is looking to see if the drug would work for other autoimmune disorders like SLE and ITP. The risk is that even though the drug was shown to do well in terms of B-cell depletion and efficacy in treating patients with pMN, there is no guarantee that it will do well in the targeting of these other two disorders. Nor that the data to be released in the 2nd half of 2026 from both of these indications will be enough to move forward with the next stage of clinical testing.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of dosing options for budoprutug. The reason why is because the company has been able to do quite well using an IV version of this drug to treat pMN. The hope is that it is going to be able to also develop a potent SC formulation for patients, one that could be offered as a self-administered at-home treatment option to treat their autoimmune disorders. Data from a phase 1 healthy volunteer study using an SC version of budoprotug is expected in the 1st half of 2026.

The risk here is that there is no assurance this SC formulation of the drug will be safe/tolerable, for starters. The company expects to release B-cell depletion data as well. Thus, another risk is that even though this drug has been set up to have B-cell depletion across the entire lineage of B-cells, there is no guarantee that final B-cell depletion data will end up meeting or exceeding expectations.

Conclusion

Eliem Therapeutics had decided to change its name over to Climb Bio. This move came about after it had acquired Tenet Medicines and also achieved a $120 million private placement agreement. The reason for the name change is to indicate a change in being able to help improve options for patients with autoimmune disorders. Its lead candidate, anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody budoprutug, is being developed in the ongoing phase 2 PrisMN study targeting pMN patients with persistent proteinuria despite optimized renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system [RAAS] inhibition.

Data from this study is expected to be released in the 2nd half of 2026. The beauty of this company is its ability to apply budoprutug to treat other B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders [Climb Bio Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #7]. As you can see from this slide, the possibilities for it beyond SLE and ITP are endless. Lastly, it is in the process of developing the anti-APRIL monoclonal antibody CLYM116 for the treatment of patients with IgA Nephropathy [IgAN].

This is being evaluated in a phase 1 healthy volunteer study and is looking at several parameters like safety, PK, and PD profile of this drug. Initial healthy volunteer data from this study is expected in mid-2026. With both positive phase 1b and long-term data achieved using budoprutug for the treatment of patients with pMN, plus a data readout from the phase 2 PrisMN study for this same program in the 2nd half of 2026, I believe that I should maintain this stock with a Buy rating.
